title: Your SaaS Channel Needs This YouTube Strategy

segments.text: If you're a SAS marketer and you're not
fully leveraging YouTube, you're leaving
serious growth on the table. Here's what
we're covering. How to build a YouTube
strategy for SAS brands. Not just get
views, but drive growth. The three
content types every SAS brand needs and
the fatal mistake most companies make.
How to actually get people to view your
content because creating great content
is just half the game. Getting it seen
is the other, plus some bonus tips to
make the content stand out. So, let's
dive in. How to build a YouTube strategy
for SAS brands. Stop treating YouTube
like a dumping ground. Many SAS teams
use their channel for a repository for
webinars, explainers, and ads. Nobody
really will care if your channel is just
a graveyard of underperforming videos.
GitHub had this challenge. They had many
different departments that were all
creating content, [music] and it became
disconnected with the overall strategy
and the performance. It ultimately
flatlined. So, we gather data on what
their audience was actually searching
for, then built a strategy around it.
YouTube isn't just for entertainment. It
is intent driven. People come here
looking for solutions, skills, and
answers. And with the right strategy,
your videos don't just get views. They
can pre-qualified leads and build trust
at scale. Unlike ads, Evergreen content

to learn with GitHub was what their
potential customers were searching for.
To do that, we had to define their ideal

means asking simple questions. Who are

YouTube gives you a gold mine of

why we start almost every client project
with the channel analysis. We study
their existing audience, their top

what your ideal viewer is really
searching for. If you're guessing
content topics, you're already behind.
And there is a wealth of data in looking

audience's attention. We like to use Vid

Take GitHub for example before their

minimal funnel. So, here is where you

your audience knows who you are, but

It's your time to show your audience why

has continued to create straightforward
demos of their product [music] instead
of just telling people what GitHub could

The three types of content SAS brands
need. Authority building content. This
is where you become the go-to voice in
your niche. [music] So, at the top of
funnel, your goal isn't to pitch your
product. It's to teach, guide, and lead
conversations that matter in your
industry. This is authoritybuilding
content and answers the [music]
questions your audience is already
searching for while subtly positioning
your brand as the expert they can trust.
The formula is simple. Identify the
conversations your buyers care about.
What are they googling? What are the big
trends or recurring challenges in your
industry? Create deep dive
problemsolving videos. These should
break down complex ideas into simple
actionable [music] insights. We've seen
the strategy work with clients like
Exabeam, who ranked in the number one
and number two spot on both Google and
YouTube for one of their most important
search terms, what is SIM? That single
video doesn't just bring them new
traffic, it positions them as a trusted
voice in the cyber security space. When
you build this type of [music] content,
you're not just answering questions,
you're shaping the way people think
about the [music] problem itself. And
once they trust you to explain the
problem, you're the natural choice to
provide the solution. problem aware to
product aare or bridge content. The
second type of content is what we call
bridge content. It helps your audience
move from simply knowing they have a
problem [music] to understanding how
your product could be the solution. The
key here is showing, not selling.
[music]
Instead of telling people your product
is great, you highlight real situations,
strategies, and stories where it plays a
role. Now you're starting to connect the
dots back to your solution. Formats
[music] that work especially well
include mini case studies that highlight
a single client or use case, strategy
breakdowns where you unpack how a
specific workflow or challenge can be
solved, and tearown style videos where
you analyze a process, tool, or approach
and then introduce how your product
streamlines it. A great example of this
comes from GitHub. They create a series
exploring real world use cases where
GitHub played a role far beyond just
storing code. From helping firefighters
coordinate during wildfires to improving
global health care access and [music]
democratizing coding education, these
stories reframe GitHub as the backbone
of collaboration and innovation across
industries. That's what bridge content
does. It educates, inspires, and it
plants the seeds that your solution is
the missing link without ever feeling
like a sales pitch. The third type is
conversion content where you stop
hinting and start asking for the
conversion. Here's where most SAS
businesses miss the mark. Why? Because
the content isn't explicitly calling the
audience to action. You need direct
content that targets decision makers.
Your content should cover things like
product comparisons, walkthroughs
showing how it actually works, use case
demos tailored to specific industries or
pain points. These videos should be
optimized for search around competitor
names, feature comparisons, or niche
keywords. So, when prospects are ready
to buy, you're right in front of them.
Okay, you've made it this far into the

[music] The key is to deliver value and
tell a story. Viewers come to YouTube

feel real, and there's a clear outcome.
That's how you separate yourself from

YouTube promotes videos that people

beats, a case study, a stat, or even a

Now, the most important thing is to make

because creating great content is just

[music] over keywords and SEO while

[music] browse feed. Audience retention,
click-through rate matter more than
metadata and SEO. That is why packaging
is critical. There are two elements to
this. Titles and thumbnails. They both
work together to deliver promise to the

the script of the video to deliver on
that promise. The thumbnail may actually
be the most important part of your
video. Most traditional designers
struggle with this. And while it's
important to be on brand, most designers
and brand managers are so scared of
stepping outside of that that they
massively sacrifice performance with
ineffective thumbnails that don't peak
interest or curiosity. [music]
We even recommend split testing
thumbnails as it's such an important

gap. that is to spark enough interest in

Finally, do not stop at YouTube. The

Retargeting viewers with a follow-up
